{{ill|Q11545372|ltq=Q11545372|WD=Q11545372|1=Senior Sixth Rank}} * {{ill|Q11071125|ltq=Q11071125|WD=Q11071125|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9122005001861 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '大阪府八尾市本町7丁目7-27', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:八尾神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11390855}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yao Shrine | 画像=[[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka01.JPG|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}Honmachi 7-7-27 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | 別名=Gozu Tennō-sha | 例祭=October 15 | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yao Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yao Shrine''' (やおじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "'''Kurusu Shrine'''" in Wakae District of {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}), and its rank was former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} of the former villages of Nishi-gō and Kido in Wakae District (now roughly the north side of Honmachi 5-chōme, Honmachi 6-7 chōme, and Higashi-Honmachi 3-chōme in Yao){{efn|The shrine's former address notation was Yao-shi Ōaza Nishi-gō 1064; east of the precincts is the Nishi-gō district community hall.}}. === Shrine name === In the early modern period it was called "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}," but after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed "Kurusu Shrine" in accordance with the Engishiki, then renamed again to the present name on April 15, Meiji 41 (1908). "{{ill|lt=Kurusu|Q136002285|WD=Q136002285|1=栗栖}}" is thought to have been the old name of the surrounding area. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Umashimaji|Q10946169|WD=Q10946169|lt=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto|1=Umashimaji}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} In the early modern period, as the name Gozu Tennō-sha indicates, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} was worshipped as deity. Since ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawachi-shi|1=UNKNOWN}}'' of {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 20 (1735), however, it has been identified with the shikinaisha "Kurusu Shrine." "Kurusu" is considered connected to the ancient {{ill|lt=clan|Q211503|WD=Q211503|1=Clan}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kurusu no Muraji|1=UNKNOWN}} who had this area as their {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}, and thus the deity came to be regarded as their ancestral deity Umashimaji-no-Mikoto, as in ''{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' (Kawachi no Kuni, section of kami-descended clans, Kurusu no Muraji). After Meiji, the deity was formally changed to Umashimaji-no-Mikoto. Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto is the deity of Hachiman Shrine of former Kido village, merged in Meiji 5. === History === The shrine site lies along {{ill|lt=Nagase River (Osaka)|Q11653384|WD=Q11653384|lt=Nagase River|1=Nagase River}} (old {{ill|lt=Yamato River|Q9001527|WD=Q9001527|1=Yamato River}}), on slightly elevated land where a settlement likely existed from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.. Though founding date is unknown, as the Ato area of Yao had Moriya no Mononobe's villa of Ato (recorded in {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, entry for 587), Yao is deeply connected with the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}} and related groups; it is considered to have begun when the Kurusu no Muraji, kin of Mononobe and based here, enshrined their ancestral deity, dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}. On April 12 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 4 ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}), it was promoted from Senior Sixth Rank Upper to Junior Fifth Rank Lower, and on Nov 11 that year was made an official shrine (''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it was listed as a government-offering small shrine. Details after the medieval period are unclear, but the surrounding area was called Yao-no-shō and this shrine is thought to have been revered as its central shrine. In the Nanboku-chō period, monk Kenkō of nearby {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (called "Yao Bettō") joined the Southern Court and held {{ill|lt=Yao Castle|Q11390786|WD=Q11390786|1=Yao Castle}}, but after the Northern Court took the castle following the Battle of Minatogawa, Southern forces attacked in {{ill|lt=Engen|Q1194159|WD=Q1194159|1=Engen}} 2 (1337), bringing war fire. This shrine may then have served as Jōkō-ji's guardian and likely suffered damage as part of the castle complex. Yao-no-shō was again a war zone in the late Muromachi period during internal conflict of the {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama clan|Q662881|WD=Q662881|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, and likely suffered again. In early modern times it was the ujigami of Nishi-gō village as Gozu Tennō-sha with Tendoku-ji as its {{ill|lt=jingūji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. Following early Meiji separation orders, Tendoku-ji was abolished and the shrine renamed Kurusu Shrine; in April Meiji 5 (1872) Hachiman Shrine of Kido village was merged, and it became village shrine of both villages. On April 15, Meiji 41, Katsura Shrine (Kaya-buri village) and Yasaka Shrine (Yao Nakano) were merged and the shrine took its current name; after the war, both shrines were restored separately. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka04.JPG|thumb|right|200px|From right (north): honden, heiden, haiden]] [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka03.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Yao Castle site monument (left) and Sugawara Shrine (right)]] The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, rebuilt in mid-Meiji. The {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is a 1-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with gable entrance, built in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 (1924), connected to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. The haiden is a 5-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} hira-iri building from {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 12 (1841). The heiden and haiden are tile-roofed. There are also a mikoshi storehouse and shrine office. === Precincts === There are two precinct shrines: Uguisu Shrine and Sugawara Shrine. On the east side of the honden, beside Sugawara Shrine, stands a stone monument to Yao Castle erected in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 6 (1931). === Access === * About 5 minutes west from {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|1=Osaka Line}}{{ill|lt=Kintetsu Yao Station|Q6414378|WD=Q6414378|lt=Yao Station|1=Kintetsu Yao Station}} === Related items === * Hatsuhiyama {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (Yao Jizōson) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://www.yao-tenmangu.com/ Official] * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Myōhō-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}[https://yaokami.jp/1277253/] * Yaosan {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chisū-in|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://tisouin.org/enkaku.html Official] * {{ill|lt=Tennō Shrine|Q11443028|WD=Q11443028|1=Tenno Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Yao (Yao city)|Q11390778|WD=Q11390778|1=Yao}} * {{ill|lt=Yahagi Shrine (Yao)|Q11583922|WD=Q11583922|1=Yahagi Shrine, Yao}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} === References === {{Commons|Category:Yao-jinja}} * Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'', Vol.4 Kawachi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1979 {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:栗栖氏]] [[Category:若江郡|社 やおしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:八尾 (八尾市)]]

{'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto 名称=Yao Shrine | 画像=[[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka01.JPG|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}Honmachi 7-7-27 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | 別名=Gozu Tennō-sha | 例祭=October 15 | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yao Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yao Shrine''' (やおじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "'''Kurusu Shrine'''" in Wakae District of {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}), and its rank was former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} of the former villages of Nishi-gō and Kido in Wakae District (now roughly the north side of Honmachi 5-chōme, Honmachi 6-7 chōme, and Higashi-Honmachi 3-chōme in Yao){{efn|The shrine's former address notation was Yao-shi Ōaza Nishi-gō 1064; east of the precincts is the Nishi-gō district community hall.}}. === Shrine name === In the early modern period it was called "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}," but after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed "Kurusu Shrine" in accordance with the Engishiki, then renamed again to the present name on April 15, Meiji 41 (1908). "{{ill|lt=Kurusu|Q136002285|WD=Q136002285|1=栗栖}}" is thought to have been the old name of the surrounding area. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Umashimaji|Q10946169|WD=Q10946169|lt=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto|1=Umashimaji}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} In the early modern period, as the name Gozu Tennō-sha indicates, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} was worshipped as deity. Since ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawachi-shi|1=UNKNOWN}}'' of {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 20 (1735), however, it has been identified with the shikinaisha "Kurusu Shrine." "Kurusu" is considered connected to the ancient {{ill|lt=clan|Q211503|WD=Q211503|1=Clan}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kurusu no Muraji|1=UNKNOWN}} who had this area as their {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}, and thus the deity came to be regarded as their ancestral deity Umashimaji-no-Mikoto, as in ''{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' (Kawachi no Kuni, section of kami-descended clans, Kurusu no Muraji). After Meiji, the deity was formally changed to Umashimaji-no-Mikoto. Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto is the deity of Hachiman Shrine of former Kido village, merged in Meiji 5. === History === The shrine site lies along {{ill|lt=Nagase River (Osaka)|Q11653384|WD=Q11653384|lt=Nagase River|1=Nagase River}} (old {{ill|lt=Yamato River|Q9001527|WD=Q9001527|1=Yamato River}}), on slightly elevated land where a settlement likely existed from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.. Though founding date is unknown, as the Ato area of Yao had Moriya no Mononobe's villa of Ato (recorded in {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, entry for 587), Yao is deeply connected with the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}} and related groups; it is considered to have begun when the Kurusu no Muraji, kin of Mononobe and based here, enshrined their ancestral deity, dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}. On April 12 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 4 ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}), it was promoted from Senior Sixth Rank Upper to Junior Fifth Rank Lower, and on Nov 11 that year was made an official shrine (''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it was listed as a government-offering small shrine. Details after the medieval period are unclear, but the surrounding area was called Yao-no-shō and this shrine is thought to have been revered as its central shrine. In the Nanboku-chō period, monk Kenkō of nearby {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (called "Yao Bettō") joined the Southern Court and held {{ill|lt=Yao Castle|Q11390786|WD=Q11390786|1=Yao Castle}}, but after the Northern Court took the castle following the Battle of Minatogawa, Southern forces attacked in {{ill|lt=Engen|Q1194159|WD=Q1194159|1=Engen}} 2 (1337), bringing war fire. This shrine may then have served as Jōkō-ji's guardian and likely suffered damage as part of the castle complex. Yao-no-shō was again a war zone in the late Muromachi period during internal conflict of the {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama clan|Q662881|WD=Q662881|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, and likely suffered again. In early modern times it was the ujigami of Nishi-gō village as Gozu Tennō-sha with Tendoku-ji as its {{ill|lt=jingūji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. Following early Meiji separation orders, Tendoku-ji was abolished and the shrine renamed Kurusu Shrine; in April Meiji 5 (1872) Hachiman Shrine of Kido village was merged, and it became village shrine of both villages. On April 15, Meiji 41, Katsura Shrine (Kaya-buri village) and Yasaka Shrine (Yao Nakano) were merged and the shrine took its current name; after the war, both shrines were restored separately. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka04.JPG|thumb|right|200px|From right (north): honden, heiden, haiden]] [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka03.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Yao Castle site monument (left) and Sugawara Shrine (right)]] The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, rebuilt in mid-Meiji. The {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is a 1-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with gable entrance, built in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 (1924), connected to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. The haiden is a 5-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} hira-iri building from {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 12 (1841). The heiden and haiden are tile-roofed. There are also a mikoshi storehouse and shrine office. === Precincts === There are two precinct shrines: Uguisu Shrine and Sugawara Shrine. On the east side of the honden, beside Sugawara Shrine, stands a stone monument to Yao Castle erected in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 6 (1931). === Access === * About 5 minutes west from {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|1=Osaka Line}}{{ill|lt=Kintetsu Yao Station|Q6414378|WD=Q6414378|lt=Yao Station|1=Kintetsu Yao Station}} === Related items === * Hatsuhiyama {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (Yao Jizōson) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://www.yao-tenmangu.com/ Official] * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Myōhō-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}[https://yaokami.jp/1277253/] * Yaosan {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chisū-in|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://tisouin.org/enkaku.html Official] * {{ill|lt=Tennō Shrine|Q11443028|WD=Q11443028|1=Tenno Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Yao (Yao city)|Q11390778|WD=Q11390778|1=Yao}} * {{ill|lt=Yahagi Shrine (Yao)|Q11583922|WD=Q11583922|1=Yahagi Shrine, Yao}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} === References === {{Commons|Category:Yao-jinja}} * Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'', Vol.4 Kawachi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1979 {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:栗栖氏]] [[Category:若江郡|社 やおしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:八尾 (八尾市)]]

Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto is the deity of Hachiman Shrine of former Kido village, merged in Meiji 5. === History === The shrine site lies along {{ill|lt=Nagase River (Osaka)|Q11653384|WD=Q11653384|lt=Nagase River|1=Nagase River}} (old {{ill|lt=Yamato River|Q9001527|WD=Q9001527|1=Yamato River}}), on slightly elevated land where a settlement likely existed from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.. Though founding date is unknown, as the Ato area of Yao had Moriya no Mononobe's villa of Ato (recorded in {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, entry for 587), Yao is deeply connected with the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}} and related groups; it is considered to have begun when the Kurusu no Muraji, kin of Mononobe and based here, enshrined their ancestral deity, dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}. On April 12 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 4 ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}), it was promoted from Senior Sixth Rank Upper to Junior Fifth Rank Lower, and on Nov 11 that year was made an official shrine (''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it was listed as a government-offering small shrine. Details after the medieval period are unclear, but the surrounding area was called Yao-no-shō and this shrine is thought to have been revered as its central shrine. In the Nanboku-chō period, monk Kenkō of nearby {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (called "Yao Bettō") joined the Southern Court and held {{ill|lt=Yao Castle|Q11390786|WD=Q11390786|1=Yao Castle}}, but after the Northern Court took the castle following the Battle of Minatogawa, Southern forces attacked in {{ill|lt=Engen|Q1194159|WD=Q1194159|1=Engen}} 2 (1337), bringing war fire. This shrine may then have served as Jōkō-ji's guardian and likely suffered damage as part of the castle complex. Yao-no-shō was again a war zone in the late Muromachi period during internal conflict of the {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama clan|Q662881|WD=Q662881|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, and likely suffered again. In early modern times it was the ujigami of Nishi-gō village as Gozu Tennō-sha with Tendoku-ji as its {{ill|lt=jingūji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. Following early Meiji separation orders, Tendoku-ji was abolished and the shrine renamed Kurusu Shrine; in April Meiji 5 (1872) Hachiman Shrine of Kido village was merged, and it became village shrine of both villages. On April 15, Meiji 41, Katsura Shrine (Kaya-buri village) and Yasaka Shrine (Yao Nakano) were merged and the shrine took its current name; after the war, both shrines were restored separately. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka04.JPG|thumb|right|200px|From right (north): honden, heiden, haiden]] [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka03.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Yao Castle site monument (left) and Sugawara Shrine (right)]] The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, rebuilt in mid-Meiji. The {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is a 1-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with gable entrance, built in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 (1924), connected to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. The haiden is a 5-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} hira-iri building from {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 12 (1841). The heiden and haiden are tile-roofed. There are also a mikoshi storehouse and shrine office. === Precincts === There are two precinct shrines: Uguisu Shrine and Sugawara Shrine. On the east side of the honden, beside Sugawara Shrine, stands a stone monument to Yao Castle erected in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 6 (1931). === Access === * About 5 minutes west from {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|1=Osaka Line}}{{ill|lt=Kintetsu Yao Station|Q6414378|WD=Q6414378|lt=Yao Station|1=Kintetsu Yao Station}} === Related items === * Hatsuhiyama {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (Yao Jizōson) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://www.yao-tenmangu.com/ Official] * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Myōhō-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}[https://yaokami.jp/1277253/] * Yaosan {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chisū-in|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://tisouin.org/enkaku.html Official] * {{ill|lt=Tennō Shrine|Q11443028|WD=Q11443028|1=Tenno Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Yao (Yao city)|Q11390778|WD=Q11390778|1=Yao}} * {{ill|lt=Yahagi Shrine (Yao)|Q11583922|WD=Q11583922|1=Yahagi Shrine, Yao}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} === References === {{Commons|Category:Yao-jinja}} * Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'', Vol.4 Kawachi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1979 {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:栗栖氏]] [[Category:若江郡|社 やおしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:八尾 (八尾市)]]

名称=Yao Shrine | 画像=[[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka01.JPG|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}Honmachi 7-7-27 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | 別名=Gozu Tennō-sha | 例祭=October 15 | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yao Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yao Shrine''' (やおじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "'''Kurusu Shrine'''" in Wakae District of {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}), and its rank was former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} of the former villages of Nishi-gō and Kido in Wakae District (now roughly the north side of Honmachi 5-chōme, Honmachi 6-7 chōme, and Higashi-Honmachi 3-chōme in Yao){{efn|The shrine's former address notation was Yao-shi Ōaza Nishi-gō 1064; east of the precincts is the Nishi-gō district community hall.}}. === Shrine name === In the early modern period it was called "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}," but after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed "Kurusu Shrine" in accordance with the Engishiki, then renamed again to the present name on April 15, Meiji 41 (1908). "{{ill|lt=Kurusu|Q136002285|WD=Q136002285|1=栗栖}}" is thought to have been the old name of the surrounding area. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Umashimaji|Q10946169|WD=Q10946169|lt=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto|1=Umashimaji}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} In the early modern period, as the name Gozu Tennō-sha indicates, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} was worshipped as deity. Since ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawachi-shi|1=UNKNOWN}}'' of {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 20 (1735), however, it has been identified with the shikinaisha "Kurusu Shrine." "Kurusu" is considered connected to the ancient {{ill|lt=clan|Q211503|WD=Q211503|1=Clan}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kurusu no Muraji|1=UNKNOWN}} who had this area as their {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}, and thus the deity came to be regarded as their ancestral deity Umashimaji-no-Mikoto, as in ''{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' (Kawachi no Kuni, section of kami-descended clans, Kurusu no Muraji). After Meiji, the deity was formally changed to Umashimaji-no-Mikoto. Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto is the deity of Hachiman Shrine of former Kido village, merged in Meiji 5. === History === The shrine site lies along {{ill|lt=Nagase River (Osaka)|Q11653384|WD=Q11653384|lt=Nagase River|1=Nagase River}} (old {{ill|lt=Yamato River|Q9001527|WD=Q9001527|1=Yamato River}}), on slightly elevated land where a settlement likely existed from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.. On April 12 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 4 ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}), it was promoted from Senior Sixth Rank Upper to Junior Fifth Rank Lower, and on Nov 11 that year was made an official shrine (''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it was listed as a government-offering small shrine. Details after the medieval period are unclear, but the surrounding area was called Yao-no-shō and this shrine is thought to have been revered as its central shrine. In the Nanboku-chō period, monk Kenkō of nearby {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (called "Yao Bettō") joined the Southern Court and held {{ill|lt=Yao Castle|Q11390786|WD=Q11390786|1=Yao Castle}}, but after the Northern Court took the castle following the Battle of Minatogawa, Southern forces attacked in {{ill|lt=Engen|Q1194159|WD=Q1194159|1=Engen}} 2 (1337), bringing war fire. This shrine may then have served as Jōkō-ji's guardian and likely suffered damage as part of the castle complex. Yao-no-shō was again a war zone in the late Muromachi period during internal conflict of the {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama clan|Q662881|WD=Q662881|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, and likely suffered again. In early modern times it was the ujigami of Nishi-gō village as Gozu Tennō-sha with Tendoku-ji as its {{ill|lt=jingūji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. Following early Meiji separation orders, Tendoku-ji was abolished and the shrine renamed Kurusu Shrine; in April Meiji 5 (1872) Hachiman Shrine of Kido village was merged, and it became village shrine of both villages. On April 15, Meiji 41, Katsura Shrine (Kaya-buri village) and Yasaka Shrine (Yao Nakano) were merged and the shrine took its current name; after the war, both shrines were restored separately. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka04.JPG|thumb|right|200px|From right (north): honden, heiden, haiden]] [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka03.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Yao Castle site monument (left) and Sugawara Shrine (right)]] The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, rebuilt in mid-Meiji. The {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is a 1-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with gable entrance, built in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 (1924), connected to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. The haiden is a 5-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} hira-iri building from {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 12 (1841). The heiden and haiden are tile-roofed. There are also a mikoshi storehouse and shrine office. === Precincts === There are two precinct shrines: Uguisu Shrine and Sugawara Shrine. On the east side of the honden, beside Sugawara Shrine, stands a stone monument to Yao Castle erected in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 6 (1931). === Access === * About 5 minutes west from {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|1=Osaka Line}}{{ill|lt=Kintetsu Yao Station|Q6414378|WD=Q6414378|lt=Yao Station|1=Kintetsu Yao Station}} === Related items === * Hatsuhiyama {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (Yao Jizōson) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://www.yao-tenmangu.com/ Official] * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Myōhō-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}[https://yaokami.jp/1277253/] * Yaosan {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chisū-in|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://tisouin.org/enkaku.html Official] * {{ill|lt=Tennō Shrine|Q11443028|WD=Q11443028|1=Tenno Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Yao (Yao city)|Q11390778|WD=Q11390778|1=Yao}} * {{ill|lt=Yahagi Shrine (Yao)|Q11583922|WD=Q11583922|1=Yahagi Shrine, Yao}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} === References === {{Commons|Category:Yao-jinja}} * Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'', Vol.4 Kawachi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1979 {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:栗栖氏]] [[Category:若江郡|社 やおしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:Yao (Yao City)]]

Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9122005001861 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '大阪府八尾市本町7丁目7-27', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:八尾神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11390855}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yao Shrine | 画像=[[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka01.JPG|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}Honmachi 7-7-27 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | 別名=Gozu Tennō-sha | 例祭=October 15 | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yao Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yao Shrine''' (やおじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872|1=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "'''Kurusu Shrine'''" in Wakae District of {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}), and its rank was former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} of the former villages of Nishi-gō and Kido in Wakae District (now roughly the north side of Honmachi 5-chōme, Honmachi 6-7 chōme, and Higashi-Honmachi 3-chōme in Yao){{efn|The shrine's former address notation was Yao-shi Ōaza Nishi-gō 1064; east of the precincts is the Nishi-gō district community hall.}}. === Shrine name === In the early modern period it was called "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}," but after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed "Kurusu Shrine" in accordance with the Engishiki, then renamed again to the present name on April 15, Meiji 41 (1908). "{{ill|lt=Kurusu|Q136002285|WD=Q136002285|1=栗栖}}" is thought to have been the old name of the surrounding area. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Umashimaji|Q10946169|WD=Q10946169|lt=Umashimaji-no-Mikoto|1=Umashimaji}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} In the early modern period, as the name Gozu Tennō-sha indicates, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} was worshipped as deity. Since ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawachi-shi|1=UNKNOWN}}'' of {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 20 (1735), however, it has been identified with the shikinaisha "Kurusu Shrine." "Kurusu" is considered connected to the ancient {{ill|lt=clan|Q211503|WD=Q211503|1=Clan}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kurusu no Muraji|1=UNKNOWN}} who had this area as their {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}, and thus the deity came to be regarded as their ancestral deity Umashimaji-no-Mikoto, as in ''{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' (Kawachi no Kuni, section of kami-descended clans, Kurusu no Muraji). After Meiji, the deity was formally changed to Umashimaji-no-Mikoto. Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto is the deity of Hachiman Shrine of former Kido village, merged in Meiji 5. === History === The shrine site lies along {{ill|lt=Nagase River (Osaka)|Q11653384|WD=Q11653384|lt=Nagase River|1=Nagase River}} (old {{ill|lt=Yamato River|Q9001527|WD=Q9001527|1=Yamato River}}), on slightly elevated land where a settlement likely existed from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.. Though founding date is unknown, as the Ato area of Yao had Moriya no Mononobe's villa of Ato (recorded in {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, entry for 587), Yao is deeply connected with the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}} and related groups; it is considered to have begun when the Kurusu no Muraji, kin of Mononobe and based here, enshrined their ancestral deity, dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}. On April 12 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 4 ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}), it was promoted from Senior Sixth Rank Upper to Junior Fifth Rank Lower, and on Nov 11 that year was made an official shrine (''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it was listed as a government-offering small shrine. Details after the medieval period are unclear, but the surrounding area was called Yao-no-shō and this shrine is thought to have been revered as its central shrine. In the Nanboku-chō period, monk Kenkō of nearby {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (called "Yao Bettō") joined the Southern Court and held {{ill|lt=Yao Castle|Q11390786|WD=Q11390786|1=Yao Castle}}, but after the Northern Court took the castle following the Battle of Minatogawa, Southern forces attacked in {{ill|lt=Engen|Q1194159|WD=Q1194159|1=Engen}} 2 (1337), bringing war fire. This shrine may then have served as Jōkō-ji's guardian and likely suffered damage as part of the castle complex. Yao-no-shō was again a war zone in the late Muromachi period during internal conflict of the {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama clan|Q662881|WD=Q662881|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, and likely suffered again. In early modern times it was the ujigami of Nishi-gō village as Gozu Tennō-sha with Tendoku-ji as its {{ill|lt=jingūji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. Following early Meiji separation orders, Tendoku-ji was abolished and the shrine renamed Kurusu Shrine; in April Meiji 5 (1872) Hachiman Shrine of Kido village was merged, and it became village shrine of both villages. On April 15, Meiji 41, Katsura Shrine (Kaya-buri village) and Yasaka Shrine (Yao Nakano) were merged and the shrine took its current name; after the war, both shrines were restored separately. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka04.JPG|thumb|right|200px|From right (north): honden, heiden, haiden]] [[File:Yao Jinja, Yao, Osaka03.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Yao Castle site monument (left) and Sugawara Shrine (right)]] The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, rebuilt in mid-Meiji. The {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is a 1-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with gable entrance, built in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 (1924), connected to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. The haiden is a 5-by-3 bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} hira-iri building from {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 12 (1841). The heiden and haiden are tile-roofed. There are also a mikoshi storehouse and shrine office. === Precincts === There are two precinct shrines: Uguisu Shrine and Sugawara Shrine. On the east side of the honden, beside Sugawara Shrine, stands a stone monument to Yao Castle erected in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 6 (1931). === Access === * About 5 minutes west from {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|1=Osaka Line}}{{ill|lt=Kintetsu Yao Station|Q6414378|WD=Q6414378|lt=Yao Station|1=Kintetsu Yao Station}} === Related items === * Hatsuhiyama {{ill|lt=Jōkō-ji (Yao)|Q25248064|WD=Q25248064|lt=Jōkō-ji|1=Jōkō-ji (Yao)}} (Yao Jizōson) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://www.yao-tenmangu.com/ Official] * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yao Myōhō-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}[https://yaokami.jp/1277253/] * Yaosan {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chisū-in|1=UNKNOWN}}[http://tisouin.org/enkaku.html Official] * {{ill|lt=Tennō Shrine|Q11443028|WD=Q11443028|1=Tenno Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Yao (Yao city)|Q11390778|WD=Q11390778|1=Yao}} * {{ill|lt=Yahagi Shrine (Yao)|Q11583922|WD=Q11583922|1=Yahagi Shrine, Yao}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} === References === {{Commons|Category:Yao-jinja}} * Shikinaisha Research Society (ed.), ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'', Vol.4 Kawachi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1979 {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yao Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:栗栖氏]] [[Category:若江郡|社 やおしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Yao, Osaka]] [[Category:Yao (Yao City)]]
